exec_summary: CWDB is cash-positive ($6,698 YTD) but the money is coming from sub-labor, not the lead engine: July has $0 booked and June closed 0 of 8 estimates. The lead engine buys attention cheaply ($168/lead); the business cannot yet convert it. Both binding constraints are self-inflicted and fixable this week: the missing license (blocking $3,225 in invoices and a $7,751 contract) and a follow-up process that has never been diagnosed. Board verdict: the license course is the highest ROI hours available and starts today; the 5 waiting leads get called only after the 8 dead estimates are autopsied, with outreach led by the small stain/resurface profile that actually closes. Ads optimization is frozen until the repaired conversion signal proves itself. The fallback for the August job gets signed terms now. Two workloads this week: the course, and the phone.
ceo_brief: CURRENT STATE: Bookings and cash tell opposite stories, and cash is winning. Booked revenue is thin (June $2,800, July $0 booked) but collections are 100 percent and YTD net income is $6,698 with the tax reserve funded. Garcia sub-labor at $6,000 is the single largest revenue line. The lead engine works: 19 clean lifetime leads, July CPL $168 (well under the $300-500 construction ceiling), half the book A-tier. The constraints are self-inflicted: (a) unlicensed status holding $3,225 of signed-demand invoices plus the Quinn HIC, and (b) follow-up throughput, with five A-tier leads unworked. Backlog is zero signed jobs against a Winchester sub engagement staged Aug 17-21. A dead Google conversion signal (6/10 to tonight) meant the bidder optimized blind for six weeks. THREE MOVES: (1) Jim starts the 12-hour DSPS course today; fallback: route Winchester builder-lane if cert late. (2) Work the five A-tier leads this week; three are near-clones of Overbeck; book 1-2 jobs into sub capacity. (3) Flip Google Search to Maximize Clicks (~$5 cap) until the restored conversion verifies and 30+ bank; keep Meta paused. STOP: generating demand ahead of conversion capacity; treating the signed sub agreement as progress; optimizing against a dead signal; conflating bookings with cash. Thesis: the machine makes leads; the license and the owner's follow-up hands are the bottleneck.

lens_outputs.name: Contrarian
lens_outputs.text: The fatal flaw: this brief diagnoses a throughput problem when the data screams a conversion problem. June: 8 estimates written, 0 closed. The centerpiece move feeds five more prospects into a process with a demonstrated 0% close rate: running the broken machine faster. The lead-engine claim is unearned: $168/lead with one booked job ever means cost per booked job is functionally the entire ad spend; A-tier has never predicted a close. The largest revenue line ($6,000 sub-labor) has nothing to do with the lead engine; the machine that actually pays is being a subcontractor. The builder-lane fallback resurrects the failed pay-per-bid model under deadline pressure, days before mobilization, no signed terms. Move (c) restarts optimization on an hours-old unverified fix, violating the brief's own commandment. Missing entirely: why did 8 estimates die? Price? Trust? Unlicensed status disclosed at the kitchen table? Until answered, every move is motion, not progress.

lens_outputs.name: First Principles
lens_outputs.text: The goal is booked gross profit per job from owned leads. The actual problem is conversion: 8 estimates, 0 closed; fix nothing else and you still book $0. The brief buries this inside follow-up throughput as if it were volume, when the estimates that WERE worked did not close. Working 5 more A-tier leads through the same process is repeating the experiment. Before the leads move, answer why 8 estimates died. The one job that closed fits a known profile (small, close, stain/repair): sell ONLY that profile until close rate is nonzero, and diagnose the 8 corpses before generating estimate 9. The license move is correctly gating and correctly first: $3,225 hostage to a 12-hour course is the highest ROI-per-hour action available. Move (c) is a distraction dressed as discipline: with 19 lifetime leads, ads optimization is noise; the honest move is spend less, not bid smarter. Right thesis, wrong bottleneck: it is what happens after the estimate leaves the owner's hands.

lens_outputs.name: Expansionist
lens_outputs.text: This brief mistakes housekeeping for strategy; every move is defensive. You repriced the ceiling to $300-500 because a booked job carries real margin, yet buy leads at $168 and propose throttling further: leaving 2-3x of provably affordable demand unbought. Capacity just arrived (two sub crews) while the brief books 1-2 jobs; why not four? What fills September through freeze-up? The license is not a task, it is a lever that instantly releases roughly $11,000 of demand; there should be a countdown, not a bullet point. Meta stays paused over a pixel event: a two-hour fix guarding a whole second demand channel through peak deck season; every idle week is unrecoverable in a seasonal trade. Missing: raising prices into demonstrated demand, packaging stain/resurface jobs as fast-cash volume, pre-selling fall slots. The thesis is right and the ambition is wrong: this is a moment to press, and it proposes to tidy.

lens_outputs.name: Outsider
lens_outputs.text: As a Wausau homeowner: the site said licensed when it wasn't, with fake testimonials from made-up names; around here that's lying and word travels fast; scrubbing it tonight was right, but anyone who already got a quote would feel conned. Five people asked for a quote and never got called back; contractors ghosting is hated more than high prices; those five already called somebody else. Eight estimates, zero closed: either prices are off or people don't trust you enough to sign; given the testimonial thing, bet on trust. What sounds right: one real job, done well, paid in full; that customer's referral is worth more than $30/day of ads. The plan is backwards in one spot: legal standing comes before booking jobs you can't lawfully sign. Call the five with an apology and lead with the one true story you have. (Chairman note: the advisor's held-deposit concern was based on a misreading; no customer money was collected.)

lens_outputs.name: Executor
lens_outputs.text: Executable, but only if Jim treats it as two workloads, not ten tasks. The real load: the 12-hour course and revenue calls. Everything else totals roughly 90 minutes and is noise pretending to be a plan. Day 1 first 30 min: the API key rotation (breaks dashboards until done). Then the three lead calls, morning, before anything else; leads decay by the hour. Batch the sub-hour admin into ONE 90-minute block (ads clicksheet, HubSpot tweaks, Webflow re-auth early since it unblocks a second pair of hands, attorney email, test form, iPhone check). Days 2-5: the course 2.5-3 hrs/day; file the two state forms the day it completes. Payment-method migration is the legitimate deferral if the week collapses. Single next step: rotate the key, then call the three leads before noon. Verdict: executable, 18-20 hours against a compromised week, if the admin confetti gets batched.

chairman_verdict: The brief's thesis stands; its centerpiece move and one claim fall. STANDS: the license as the first and gating move. Every advisor converged here: a 12-hour course releases $3,225 in deliberately uncollected invoices plus a $7,751 signed contract. Correction to the record: no customer money is held; those are open invoices Jim is correctly declining to collect until licensed. The discipline already exists; the board commends it and strikes any escrow concern. FALLS: work-the-5-A-tier-leads as written. The data shows a conversion failure, not a throughput failure: 8 estimates in June, 0 closed. Feeding five more prospects into an unexamined 0% process is repeating the experiment. The leads get worked, but only after a same-day autopsy of the 8 dead estimates, and the outreach leads with the one profile that has ever closed (small, close, stain/resurface, legal to sign today), opening with an apology for the silence, because trust, not price, is the likelier killer. ALSO FALLS: the ads flip. Restarting optimization on an hours-old unverified signal violates the brief's own stop-list. Freeze ad settings; verify the signal for a week first. CHANGES: the builder-lane fallback survives only if terms with the licensed partner are signed this week, not improvised days before an August mobilization. The press-harder instinct is right in direction, wrong in timing: ambition is earned by the first nonzero close rate. Scope the Meta pixel fix now, spend nothing more until conversion is proven. Two workloads this week: the course, and the phone.

recommended_moves.title: Complete DSPS course and file same day
recommended_moves.detail: Jim starts the 12-hour Dwelling Contractor course today, finishes within 5 days, and files both state forms the day it completes. This is a countdown, not a task: completion releases $3,225 in open invoices (Koy + Peksa, deliberately uncollected, correctly so) and unblocks the $7,751 Quinn contract send.

recommended_moves.title: Autopsy the 8 dead estimates, then call the 5 A-tier leads
recommended_moves.detail: Before any new estimate goes out, determine why all 8 June estimates died (price, trust, licensing disclosure, follow-up lag). Then call the 5 unworked A-tier leads before noon, opening with an apology for the delay, leading with the stain/resurface profile that matches the only job ever closed and is legal to sign today.

recommended_moves.title: Freeze ads optimization until the conversion signal verifies
recommended_moves.detail: Do not flip Google to Maximize Clicks on an hours-old signal fix. Hold current settings, verify offline conversions flow correctly for 7 days, then decide. Meta stays paused, but scope the Pixel Lead fix now so the second channel can open the week close rate goes nonzero.

recommended_moves.title: Paper the Winchester builder-lane fallback now
recommended_moves.detail: If the cert may slip past Aug 17-21, the routing of Winchester through a licensed partner as prime needs signed terms this week (fee, scope, deposit handling, permit responsibility), not a handshake days before mobilization. This de-risks the fallback without resurrecting the pay-per-bid model under pressure.

recommended_moves.title: Draft the post-first-close press plan
recommended_moves.detail: Prepare, but do not execute, the demand-side push: fall-slot pre-selling into sub capacity, stain/resurface packaged as fast-cash volume, and a pricing review against demonstrated demand. Trigger: first booked job from the current pipeline.

kpi_snapshot.session_2026_07_22: 8 of 21 audit tasks closed; site repositioned (CMS live, DOM blocked on Webflow scope); conversion signal fix shipped unverified; terminations + sub agreement drafted pending Yde
